lessons.title: Overview of the java.time Package
lessons.summary: Learn about the core classes of the java.time package introduced in Java 8. Understand how to create LocalDate, LocalTime, and LocalDateTime, and the immutable design philosophy.

contents.title: Overview of the Date & Time API
contents.body:
In this lesson, you will learn:

- The design philosophy of the java.time package (immutable, [[thread]]-safe)
- Differences between **LocalDate**, **LocalTime**, and [[LocalDateTime]]
- Creating instances using now() and of()

This is the new API that resolves the issues of the old Date/Calendar.

contents.title: LocalDate, LocalTime, and LocalDateTime
contents.body:
| Class | Description |
|---|---|
| LocalDate | Represents only year, month, and day without time information. Expressed in ISO 8601 format (yyyy-MM-dd). |
| LocalTime | Represents hours, minutes, seconds, and nanoseconds without date information. |
| LocalDateTime | Represents both date and time. |
All of these are **immutable**, and operation [[methods]] return new [[instances]].
Since the original object is not modified, changes are not reflected unless the return value is captured.
The [[constructor]] is private, so code like new LocalDate() results in a [[compile]] error.
contents.note: Not capturing the return value of an operation method, resulting in no change being reflected, is a very common trick question on exams.

contents.title: How to Create Instances
contents.body:
The now() method retrieves the current date/time.
The of() method creates an instance with specified values. The month can be specified as int or Month enum type.
The parse() method creates an instance from a string.
Specifying invalid values throws a DateTimeException (e.g., month 13, day 32).
LocalDateTime can also be created from a LocalDate and LocalTime.
contents.note: "//" in code indicates a comment (explanation text). It does not affect program execution.

contents.title: Relationship with Month and Prohibition of new
contents.body:
The month can be specified using int type (1–12) or Month enum type.
Month.JANUARY = January, Month.DECEMBER = December, and it is not zero-based.
This is an improvement over the counter-intuitive specification of the old API where Calendar.JANUARY = 0.
Direct constructor calls such as new **LocalDate**() are not allowed and result in a compile error.
By using factory methods, the creation of objects in invalid states is prevented.
contents.note: In the old Calendar API, months were zero-based (0 = January), but in java.time they are one-based (1 = January).

contents.title: Exam Prep: Date & Time Basics
contents.body:
On the Gold exam, the pattern of not capturing the return value of **immutable** operations is very common.
Also remember that instance creation with the new operator results in a compile error.
Passing a string in an invalid format to parse() causes a DateTimeParseException.
LocalDate does not have a time() method. To work with time, use LocalTime or LocalDateTime.
Also be careful with methods for converting between LocalDate and LocalTime.

quizzes.question: Which of the following is a correct description about the date and time classes in the java.time package?
quizzes.explanation: LocalDate, LocalTime, and LocalDateTime in the java.time package are all immutable. Operation methods return new instances, and the original object is not modified. Since the constructor is private, they cannot be created with new.

choices.text: All are immutable, and operation methods return new instances
They are mutable, and operation methods modify and return themselves
Instances can be created with the new operator

quizzes.question: Predict the output of the following code.
quizzes.explanation: Since LocalDate is immutable, changes are not reflected unless the return value of plusMonths(1) is captured. d remains the original 2024-01-31. This is an important characteristic of the java.time API and a frequently tested pattern on exams.

quizzes.question: To get the current date, use the LocalDate.___() method.
quizzes.explanation: LocalDate.now() is a factory method that returns the current date. of() creates an instance with a specified year, month, and day, and parse() creates an instance from a string.

lessons.title: Date and Time Operations
lessons.summary: Learn how to use addition/subtraction methods (plus/minus), field modification methods (with), and the Period and Duration classes that represent time intervals.

contents.title: How to Manipulate Date and Time
contents.body:
In this lesson, you will learn:

- Adding and subtracting dates/times with plus/minus [[methods]]
- Modifying specific fields with with methods
- Using [[Period]] (date interval) and [[Duration]] (time interval)

[[Immutable object]] operation methods return new objects.

contents.title: plus/minus Methods
contents.body:
| Class | Main Methods |
|---|---|
| LocalDate | plusDays(), plusWeeks(), plusMonths(), plusYears() |
| LocalTime | plusHours(), plusMinutes(), plusSeconds(), plusNanos() |
| LocalDateTime | All of the above methods |

When adding months to an end-of-month date, the day is automatically adjusted (e.g., Jan 31 + 1 month = Feb 28 or 29).
Multiple operations can be chained using method chaining.
contents.note: End-of-month auto-adjustment is frequently tested on Gold exams. Adding 1 month to Jan 31 gives Feb 28 (or 29).

contents.title: with Method and TemporalAdjusters
contents.body:
The with methods return a new [[instance]] with a specific field changed to the specified value.
Methods include withDayOfMonth(), withMonth(), withYear(), etc.
Specifying an invalid value throws a DateTimeException.
The TemporalAdjusters class makes it easy to perform complex adjustments like "next Monday" or "last day of the month."
contents.note: "//" in code indicates a comment (explanation text). It does not affect program execution.

contents.title: Period and Duration
contents.body:
Period represents a period in years, months, and days, and is used with LocalDate.
Duration represents a period in hours, minutes, seconds, and nanoseconds, and is used with LocalTime and [[LocalDateTime]].
Period.between() calculates the period between two dates.
Duration.between() calculates the period between two times.
Applying a Period to LocalTime or a Duration to LocalDate throws an UnsupportedTemporalTypeException.
contents.note: Remember that Period is for LocalDate and Duration is for LocalTime. Confusing them is a common exam question.

contents.title: Exam Prep: Date Operations
contents.body:
End-of-month auto-adjustment (Jan 31 + 1 month = Feb 28/29) is guaranteed to appear on exams.
Remember the distinction between Period and Duration (Period for dates, Duration for times).
Also remember that ChronoUnit.DAYS.between() can be used to calculate the number of days between two dates.
Period's toTotalMonths() method can be used to get the total number of months.
Duration's toHours() and toMinutes() methods can be used for unit conversion.

quizzes.question: Predict the output of the following code.
quizzes.explanation: 2024 is a leap year, so February has 29 days. When adding 1 month to January 31, since the 31st does not exist in February, it is automatically adjusted to the last day of February (29th). The return value is assigned back to d, so the change is reflected.

quizzes.question: Which of the following descriptions about Period and Duration is correct?
quizzes.explanation: Period represents a date-based period (years, months, days) and is used with LocalDate. Duration is used with time-based objects such as LocalTime and Instant. Applying a Period to LocalTime causes an UnsupportedTemporalTypeException.

choices.text: Period is used with LocalDate, and Duration is used with LocalTime
Period is used with LocalTime, and Duration is used with LocalDate
Both Period and Duration can be used with LocalDate
Period and Duration are aliases for the same class

quizzes.question: Predict the output of the following code.
quizzes.explanation: Period.between() calculates the period between two dates. From 2024-01-01 to 2024-03-15 is 2 months and 14 days. getMonths() returns 2 and getDays() returns 14.

quizzes.question: Complete the code to manipulate dates using LocalDate.
quizzes.explanation: LocalDate.now() retrieves the current date, and plusDays() adds the specified number of days. Since it is an immutable object, a new instance is returned.
quizzes.codeTemplate:
LocalDate today = LocalDate.__BLANK_1__();
LocalDate nextWeek = today.__BLANK_2__(7);

lessons.title: DateTimeFormatter
lessons.summary: Learn about formatting and parsing dates and times. Understand predefined formatters, how to specify custom patterns, and how to use the format() and parse() methods.

contents.title: What Is DateTimeFormatter?
contents.body:
In this lesson, you will learn:

- Predefined formatters in [[DateTimeFormatter]]
- Creating custom formats with ofPattern()
- Converting strings to date/time objects with parse()

Converting between date/time and strings is a frequently used operation in practice.

contents.title: Predefined Formatters
contents.body:
DateTimeFormatter provides predefined formatters for ISO standards.
ISO_LOCAL_DATE: yyyy-MM-dd format (e.g., 2024-03-15)
ISO_LOCAL_TIME: HH:mm:ss format (e.g., 14:30:45)
ISO_LOCAL_DATE_TIME: yyyy-MM-ddTHH:mm:ss format (e.g., 2024-03-15T14:30:45)
These are the same format as toString() of LocalDate, LocalTime, etc.
Locale-aware formatting is also possible using ofLocalizedDate() with FormatStyle.
contents.note: ISO_LOCAL_DATE_TIME uses the T separator between date and time.

contents.title: Custom Patterns (ofPattern)
contents.body:
You can specify a custom format pattern with **DateTimeFormatter**.ofPattern().
Main pattern characters: y (year), M (month), d (day), H (hour 24h), h (hour 12h), m (minute), s (second)
Pay attention to the difference between uppercase and lowercase (M is month, m is minute, H is 24-hour, h is 12-hour).
The number of repeated pattern characters determines the output digit count (yy is 2-digit year, yyyy is 4-digit year).
Characters enclosed in single quotes are output as [[literals]].
contents.note: "//" in code indicates a comment (explanation text). It does not affect program execution.

contents.title: How to Use parse()
contents.body:
The parse() [[method]] creates a date/time object from a string.
It is used in the form **LocalDate**.parse(string, formatter).
If the formatter is omitted, it parses using the ISO standard format (yyyy-MM-dd, etc.).
Passing a string that does not match the pattern throws a DateTimeParseException.
The formatter pattern and the string format must match exactly.
contents.note: parse() and format() are inverse operations. You can round-trip format → parse with the same formatter.

contents.title: Exam Prep: Format and Parse
contents.body:
On the Gold exam, you will be tested on your precise understanding of the ofPattern() pattern characters.
The difference between M and m (M = month, m = minute) and the difference between H and h (H = 24-hour, h = 12-hour) are essential.
Using a time pattern on a LocalDate throws an UnsupportedTemporalTypeException.
When parsing, if the format does not match, a DateTimeParseException occurs.
format() can be called from both the formatter and the object (dt.format(f) and f.format(dt) produce the same result).

quizzes.question: Predict the output of the following code.
quizzes.explanation: With the pattern ofPattern("yyyy/MM/dd HH:mm"), the year is displayed in 4 digits, the month and day in 2 digits (zero-padded), and the 24-hour time and minutes are also displayed in 2 digits. March is displayed as 03, the 5th as 05, 9 o'clock as 09, and 7 minutes as 07.

quizzes.question: To specify a custom pattern with DateTimeFormatter, use the DateTimeFormatter.___() method.
quizzes.explanation: You can create a custom formatter by passing a pattern string to the DateTimeFormatter.ofPattern() method. Pattern characters include y (year), M (month), d (day), H (hour), m (minute), s (second), etc.

quizzes.question: Which of the following is the correct combination of DateTimeFormatter pattern characters?
quizzes.explanation: M (uppercase) represents month, and m (lowercase) represents minute. H (uppercase) represents 24-hour format hour, and h (lowercase) represents 12-hour format hour. This uppercase/lowercase distinction is frequently tested on exams.

choices.text: M=month, m=minute, H=24-hour hour, h=12-hour hour
M=minute, m=month, H=12-hour hour, h=24-hour hour
M=month, m=minute, H=12-hour hour, h=24-hour hour
M=minute, m=month, H=24-hour hour, h=12-hour hour

quizzes.question: Select all classes that belong to the java.time package.
quizzes.explanation: LocalDate, LocalTime, Duration, and Period are classes in java.time. Calendar is a legacy API from java.util.

lessons.title: ZonedDateTime and Instant
lessons.summary: Learn about the ZonedDateTime class for handling time zones and the Instant class representing epoch-based time points. Understand how to use ZoneId and how to convert between types.

contents.title: Time Zones and Instant
contents.body:
In this lesson, you will learn:

- Handling timezone-aware date/time with [[ZonedDateTime]]
- Generating date/time from epoch seconds with [[Instant]]
- Conversion methods between [[classes]]

This is essential knowledge for global applications.

contents.title: ZoneId and ZonedDateTime
contents.body:
ZoneId is a class representing a time zone, created with ZoneId.of("Asia/Tokyo").
ZonedDateTime is [[LocalDateTime]] with a ZoneId added.
**ZonedDateTime**.now() retrieves the current timezone-aware date/time.
A ZonedDateTime can be created from a LocalDateTime and ZoneId (using the atZone() [[method]]).
The toString() of ZonedDateTime includes timezone information (offset and zone ID).
ZoneId.getAvailableZoneIds() retrieves a list of available timezone IDs.
contents.note: withZoneSameInstant() represents the same moment in a different timezone. withZoneSameLocal() changes only the zone while keeping the local time the same.

contents.title: Instant Class
contents.body:
Instant represents a point in time as the elapsed time from the epoch (1970-01-01T00:00:00Z).
Instant.now() retrieves the current moment, and Instant.ofEpochSecond() creates one from epoch seconds.
Instant has no concept of timezone and is always UTC-based.
Instant and ZonedDateTime can be converted to each other.
[[Duration]].between() can calculate the time difference between Instants.
contents.note: "//" in code indicates a comment (explanation text). It does not affect program execution.

contents.title: Summary of Type Conversions
contents.body:
| Conversion | Method |
|---|---|
| LocalDateTime → ZonedDateTime | Use atZone(ZoneId). |
| ZonedDateTime → LocalDateTime | Use toLocalDateTime(). |
| ZonedDateTime → Instant | Use toInstant(). |
| LocalDateTime → Instant | Cannot convert directly; must go through ZonedDateTime first. |
These conversion relationships are frequently tested on the Gold exam.
contents.note: Note that direct conversion from LocalDateTime to Instant is not possible.

contents.title: Exam Prep: ZonedDateTime and Instant
contents.body:
- On the Gold exam, the difference between withZoneSameInstant() and withZoneSameLocal() is tested.
- withZoneSameInstant() represents the same moment in a different zone (the time changes), while withZoneSameLocal() changes only the zone while maintaining the local time (the moment changes).
- Note that Instant does not have methods like plusDays(); use plus(Duration) to perform operations.

Organizing the [[type conversion]] paths in a diagram helps deepen understanding.
Passing an invalid zone ID to ZoneId.of() throws a ZoneRulesException.

quizzes.question: Which of the following is a correct description about Instant?
quizzes.explanation: Instant represents a point in time as the elapsed time from the epoch (1970-01-01T00:00:00Z) and is always UTC-based. It does not hold timezone information and uniquely identifies a specific moment. It can be converted to a ZonedDateTime using atZone().

choices.text: Represents a point in time as elapsed time from January 1, 1970 UTC
It is a subclass of LocalDateTime
Days can be added using the plusDays() method

quizzes.question: Predict the output of the following code.
quizzes.explanation: Converting 2024-03-15T12:00 in Tokyo (UTC+9) to UTC subtracts 9 hours, resulting in 2024-03-15T03:00:00Z. Instant's toString() displays the UTC time with a Z suffix.

quizzes.question: Select the error contained in the following code.
quizzes.explanation: Direct conversion from LocalDateTime to Instant is not possible. LocalDateTime does not have a parameterless toInstant() method (because UTC time cannot be determined without timezone information). While toInstant(ZoneOffset) exists, it cannot be called without arguments and results in a compile error. The correct approach is to convert to ZonedDateTime using atZone() first, then call toInstant().

choices.text: LocalDateTime does not have a parameterless toInstant() method
The number of arguments to LocalDateTime.of() is incorrect
A LocalDateTime value cannot be assigned to an Instant variable
Instant cannot be output with System.out.println()
